questions.explanation: <h1> represents the highest-level heading and is used for primary headings in a document or section.
questions.question: Which HTML element is most appropriate for the main heading of a page section?

questions.explanation: Padding adds internal spacing between the content box and the element’s border.
questions.question: Which CSS property is used to create space inside an element, between its content and border?

questions.explanation: GET is designed for safe, read-only retrieval of resources.
questions.question: Which HTTP method is typically used to retrieve a resource without changing server state?

questions.explanation: Due to a long-standing JavaScript quirk, typeof null returns "object".
questions.question: In JavaScript, what is the result of typeof null?

questions.explanation: PATCH is intended for partial updates, while PUT typically replaces the full resource representation.
questions.question: You want to create a REST endpoint to partially update a user's profile (e.g., only the displayName). Which method best fits?

questions.explanation: A LEFT JOIN keeps all customers and counts matching orders; customers with no orders produce NULL order rows that COUNT(o.id) treats as 0.

options.A: SELECT c.id, COUNT(o.id) FROM customers c INNER JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id GROUP BY c.id;
options.B: SELECT c.id, COUNT(o.id) FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id GROUP BY c.id;
options.C: SELECT c.id, SUM(o.id) FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.customer_id = c.id;
options.D: SELECT c.id, COUNT(*) FROM orders o RIGHT JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.id WHERE o.id IS NOT NULL GROUP BY c.id;

questions.question: Which SQL query correctly returns the number of orders per customer, including customers with zero orders?

questions.explanation: express.json() parses JSON payloads and populates req.body.
questions.question: In Node.js with Express, which middleware is commonly used to parse incoming JSON request bodies?

questions.explanation: XSS can read localStorage and exfiltrate tokens, enabling session hijacking; JWTs are not meant to be decrypted for passwords, and signature verification still applies.

options.A: The attacker can read the JWT from localStorage and impersonate the user until the token expires.
options.B: The attacker can decrypt the JWT and recover the user's password.
options.C: The attacker can bypass TLS and intercept all network traffic.
options.D: The attacker can force the server to accept any JWT without verifying its signature.

questions.question: A JWT is stored in localStorage and sent in an Authorization header. An attacker finds an XSS vulnerability on your site. What is the most likely impact?

questions.explanation: 3NF reduces redundancy by separating entities (Customers) and using foreign keys (customer_id) so customer attributes depend on the customer key, not on order_id.

options.A: Add an index on customer_email to speed up queries.
options.B: Split customer fields into a Customers table and reference it from Orders via customer_id.
options.C: Store customer_name and customer_email as a JSON column inside Orders.
options.D: Duplicate shipping_address into a second column to keep history.

questions.question: You have a table Orders(order_id, customer_name, customer_email, shipping_address, order_date). Many orders share the same customer data. Which change best moves the design toward 3NF?

questions.explanation: React onClick expects a function receiving a mouse event; wrapping lets you call onSelect with the required numeric id while keeping correct types.

options.A: Change the prop type to onSelect: (e: React.MouseEvent<HTMLButtonElement>) => void.
options.B: Wrap the call so the click handler matches React’s expected signature: <button onClick={() => onSelect(id)}>Select</button>.
options.C: Cast onSelect to any: <button onClick={onSelect as any}>Select</button>.
options.D: Disable TypeScript checking for this file using // @ts-nocheck.

questions.question: In a React + TypeScript app, you have a component prop typed as onSelect: (id: number) => void. You pass it to a button: <button onClick={onSelect}>Select</button>. What is the best fix?
